coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots modulated/polyindole(PIn/CDs) thin film by electrospray deposition for high-performance next-generation solid-state supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Dhandapani, Elumalai
kandiah, Kavitha
Vasudevan, R
Ramesh, R
Duraisamy, Navaneethan
coredata.dc:description: A binder-free polyindole/carbon quantum dots (PIn/CDs) thin-film/layer electrode is synthesized by a hybrid-electrospray method. The synthesized CDs and PIn/CDs samples are used for furthercharacterization. Owing to the synergistic consequence of PIn and CDs, PIn/CDs display outstanding electrochemicalassets and cyclability, whichare deserved from the presence of robust interactions amongst polymeric chains and CDs. Further, CDs offer an electron allocation pathway owing to their extraordinary electricconductivity and high specific surface area. In a 3 M KOH aqueous-electrolyte, the Qs of PCDs-2is394 C/g at 6 A/g, which is higher than other comparative electrodes. The PCDs-2 film shows excellent cyclicretention of 75% at 20 Ag−1 up to 3000 cycles. The fabricated PIn/CDs-2//rGO asymmetric device shows the highest specific capacity of 54.5 C/g at 1 A/g with theenergy and power density of 15.9 Wh/kg and 955.6 W/kg. Theseresults demonstrate that PIn/CDs thin-film could be used as an efficient electrode for supercapacitor application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Polyindole
Electrospray
Supercapacitor

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Section on 7th International Conference on Nanoscience and Nanotechnology-ICONN 2023

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of PIn and PIn/CDs composite

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dr. Navaneethan Duraisamy acknowledges the DST, New Delhi, India , for the DST Inspire Faculty award ( DST/INSPIRE/04/ 2018/001444 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of PIn/CDs composite synthesis.
Optical spectrum of CDs: (a) UV–Vis, (b) Photoluminescence.
XRD patterns (a) PIn, (b) PCDs-1, (c) PCDs-2, d) PCDs-3 composite.
FTIR spectra: (a) PIn, (b) PCDs-1, (c) PCDs-2, d) PCDs-3 composite.
(a) XPS survey of PIn and PIn/CDs (b, c) XPS spectra of C1s of PIn and PIn/CDs (d, e) XPS spectra of N1s of PIn and PIn/CDs, and (f) XPS spectra of O1s of PIn/CDs.
SEM images: Low magnification (a) PIn,(c)PCDs-1,(e)PCDs-2, (g) PCDs-3 High magnification: (b) PIn, (d) PCDs-1, (f) PCDs-2, (h) PCDs-3 composite.

(a) CV curves of as-prepared electrodes under the scan rate of 60 mV/s, (b) GCD curves of synthesized electrodes at 10 A/g, (c)QS Vs current density plot, (d) Cyclic stability, (e) Nyquist plot, (f) Equant circuit diagram, (g,h) Bode plots.

(a-c) Snapshots of flat and bending states of an as-prepared electrode, (d) CV curves at various bending angles undera constant scan rate of 80 mV/s, (e) GCD profiles at 12 A/g, (f) Qs at different bending angles, (g) Nyquist plot, (h,i) Bode plots.

(a) Schematic illustration of device fabrication, (b) CV curve of both positive and negative electrodes at 5 mV/s, (c) CV curves under various voltage at constant scan rate (80 mV/s), (d) GCD profiles under a various potential window at constant current density (3 A/g).

(a) GCD profilesat different current densities from 1 to 10 A/g, (b) QS of the fabricated device, (c) Ragone Plot (Energy density Vs power density), (d) Cyclic/coulombic retentionof the fabricateddevice, (e) Equivalent circuit plot,(f) Nyquist plot.
The mass loading of the synthesized electrodes.
Specific capacity comparison with previously reported works in the literature (three-electrode cell).
Rs and Rct of the synthesized ((a) PIn, (b) PCDs-1, (c) PCDs-2, d) PCDs-3 composite) electrodes.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots modulated/polyindole(PIn/CDs) thin film by electrospray deposition for high-performance next-generation solid-state supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, J.K.K. Nataraja College of Arts and Science, Komarapalayam, Namakkal, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: J.K.K. Nataraja College of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, J.K.K. Nataraja College of Arts and Science, Komarapalayam, Namakkal, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology and Biomedical Engineering, Paavai Engineering College, Pachal, Namakkal 637018, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biotechnology and Biomedical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Biotechnology, Paavai College of Technology, Paavai Institutions, Pachal, Namakkal 637018, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Universal Phi Private Limited, Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cUniversal Phi Private Limited, Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Periyar University, Salem, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dDepartment of Physics, Periyar University, Salem, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A binder-free polyindole/carbon quantum dots (PIn/CDs) thin-film/layer electrode is synthesized by a hybrid-electrospray method. The synthesized CDs and PIn/CDs samples are used for furthercharacterization. Owing to the synergistic consequence of PIn and CDs, PIn/CDs display outstanding electrochemicalassets and cyclability, whichare deserved from the presence of robust interactions amongst polymeric chains and CDs. Further, CDs offer an electron allocation pathway owing to their extraordinary electricconductivity and high specific surface area. In a 3 M KOH aqueous-electrolyte, the Qs of PCDs-2is394 C/g at 6 A/g, which is higher than other comparative electrodes. The PCDs-2 film shows excellent cyclicretention of 75% at 20 Ag−1 up to 3000 cycles. The fabricated PIn/CDs-2//rGO asymmetric device shows the highest specific capacity of 54.5 C/g at 1 A/g with theenergy and power density of 15.9 Wh/kg and 955.6 W/kg. Theseresults demonstrate that PIn/CDs thin-film could be used as an efficient electrode for supercapacitor application.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical/supercapacitors are considered auspicious energy storage devices owing to their excellent power density, fastest charging/discharging capability, and outstanding cyclability [1]. The rapid development of portable/wearable electronics and other smart electric devices need an efficient energy storage device with high energy density, high flexibility, and cost-effectiveness. Many investigators are made some achievements in improving high energy/power density, outstanding specific capacitance, and cyclability but they could not fulfill the requirements of next-generation electronics [2,3]. So, the researchers focused to make a new kind of electrode material with the above requirements. Presently carbonaceous materials, metal oxides, and conducting polymers are used as active electrode materials for supercapacitors (SCs), However, electrode materials have some disadvantages such as low conductivity of carbonaceous materials, low specific capacitance (CS) of metal oxides, and low cyclability of conducting polymers (CPs) [4,5]. These drawbacks can limit the commercialization and hands-on application of SCs. Since, a unique property of CPs attentively attracts the researchers such as good conductivity, high flexibility, high storage capability, and easy fabrication. Therefore, CPs as one of the ideal active materials for SCs, but the poor cyclability of CPs, is one of the major-drawback, to overcome these issues some other active materials are used as an additive to enhance the overall properties of CPs [6,7].Hui Peng et al. reported the synthesis of CuS@PPy composites for high-performance SCs electrodes [8]. The CS of the composite electrode is 427.5 Fg−1 at 1 Ag−1 is higher than Pristine PPy and CuS matrix 275 and 216.7 Fg−1 at 1 Ag−1 respectively CuS@PPy show edcyclic-retention of 88% after 1000 cycles, it is higher than that of pristine CuS and PPy 81% and 56% respectively. Therefore, the above literature showed the additives enhance the cyclability & specific capacitance of the pristine materials. Ramesh et al. reported that CNT/PIn electrodes for supercapacitors via facial in-situ and ex-situ methods. As synthesized CNT/PIn via in-situ showed the highest specific capacitance than ex-situ synthesized CNT/PIn. Therefore the synthesis process also plays a vital role to improve the electrochemical performance of the electrode [9].

Conducting polymers (CPs), are highly attractive electrode materials for energy storage materials, owing to their high conductivity, flexibility, easy fabrication, and cost-effectiveness. Nowadays numerous CPs are used as electrode materials for energy storage applications, such as Polyaniline, Polypyrrole, Polythiophene, and their derivatives.

However, cyclability is the major issue for hands-on application owing to the structural instability of PPy and PANI ensuring the degradation and repetitive volumetric shrinking/swelling during the charge/discharge process. Among them, PIn has a considerable one due to their outstanding properties, PIn consists of the hexagonal and pentagonal fused ring structure, where it owns the assets of both PANI and PPy together like high redox-activity, slow degradation rate, and long-cyclability as compared to PANI and PPy [10,11]. The PIn is not widely studied for SCs application due to its low electrochemical performance and low cyclability compare to PANI and PPy. The malice of this asset like tunable electrical conductivity, high-thermal steadiness, and high redox activity make PIn fairly considerable in energy storage. [12]. To overcome these dodges, make a composite with carbonaceous materials such as (activated carbon, carbon nanotube, graphene, carbon aerogel, etc.)among the various carbonaceous materials CDs have been considered due to their unique assets like good electrical conductivity, numerous active sites, high specific surface area, high-thermal steadiness, low toxicity, and well dispersed in numerous solvents [13,14].The CDs are electrostatically bonded to the polymer matrix and enhance the electrochemical performance of pristine PIn, the CDs consist the functional groups like COO−, NH, etc. these passivated surface functionalities are combined with the NH group of indole moiety, and the interaction of functional groups between the two individual matrices enhances the conductivity and stability of PIn/CDs composite

Herein we fabricate the PIn/CDs thin film/layer by one-step hybrid electrostatic spray deposition technique (HESD). HESD is widely studied for thin film-making, and the electrochemical performance of the synthesized PIn/CDs films is studied in a 3 M KOH electrolyte. CDs incorporation can improve the electrochemical performance of PIn. PIn/CDs film is anticipated to parade excellent conductivity and electrochemical performance. The two-electrode system is constructed by the well-performed PIn/CDs film, it illustrated the outstanding specific capacitance and cyclability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PIn and PIn/CDs composite

ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of synthesized CD were analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800), and photoluminescence spectra were recorded by JASCO fluorescence spectrophotometer. FTIR spectra of PIn and PCDs composite were examined by an FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA, model Spectrum-BX). XRD analysis was done by Rigaku D/Max-250 under the scan rate of 0.5° min−1 with a Cu Kα radiation source. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed with Al-Kα on a K-Alpha+ photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Escalab 250Xi).The surface morphology of the synthesized film was examined by Scanning electron microscope (SEM) (CARL ZEISS),and Transmission Electron Microscope (JEM-2100). Electrochemical characterizations were performed biological Sp-150 workstation.

Optical spectra of CDs revealed in Fig. 2 (a). UV–VIS spectra showed two peaks at 273 and 336 nm which representπ-π∗ and n-π∗transitions of aromatic CC and conjugated CO [15,16]. PL (Fig. 2(b)) spectra of CDs recorded at 340 nm.The CDs display a robust green emission at 402 nm concerning the excitation at 340 nm [17].

XRD patterns of PIn, PCDs-1, PCDs-2, and PCDs-3 composites are illustrated in Fig. 3 . XRD patterns revealed a broad diffraction peak, signifying that all as-prepared materials were amorphous. XRD pattern of pristine PIn displayed two broad diffraction peaks around 2θ = 20.7° and 2θ = 26.2°, which suggests the presence of amorphous PIn [18]. As far as PCDs composites exhibited an amorphous nature, a sign of the amorphous nature of PCDs composites and PIn. As a result, X-ray curves are shifted to minimum angles and the intensity of the peak also decreased, which denotes that the amorphous nature of PCDs is improved with increasing CD content in the composites [19]. The XRD patterns of PIn and its composites showed the peak intensity decreased based on the CDs concentration, which supports the hypothesis of physical interaction among PIn and CDs

The FTIR spectra of PIn and its composite PCDs are shown in Fig. 4 . The prepared PIn and PCDs composites showed the typical peaks at 3498 and 1564 cm-1 may be due to the NH stretching and deformation vibrations of indole-mediety, which indicated the presence of amine (NH) in the synthesized sample. The peak at 2925 and 745 cm-1 represents the stretching and out-plane bending vibrations of CH functionality. The peak at 2340 cm-1 associated with CN, denotes the presence of amino (NH2) functionality, which is essential for streptavidin conjugation [20–22]. 1622 cm-1 is allocated to the Stretching frequencies of CC, 1455 cm-1 is associated with the vibrational frequency of the benzene ring CH. The same peaks that are attained in PIn are all shown in PIn/CDs composites. The peak at 1708 and 1233 cm-1 indicates the existence of symmetry and asymmetry in carboxyl groups (COO-). The peaks have deviated in wavelength, and the intensity has decreased with increasing CDs which may be due to the synergic effect between the PIn and CDs. At certain concentrations of CDs, the PCDs-3 film peak intensity is increased, based on the hypothesis that the high concentration of CDs affects the synergic interaction. Therefore, the peak intensity could be increased. The above results, confirm the good incorporation of CDs into the polymer matrix.

The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) are used to analyze the different oxidation states and degree of functionalization of the synthesized compounds (Fig. 5 ). The XPS survey spectrum of as-prepared PIn and PIn/CDS is illustrated in Fig. 5(a), it comprises a sharp and strong peak at 283.4 and 282.6 eV ascribed to C1s electronic configuration of PIn and PIn/CDs, PIn illustrated one sharper peak at 398.6 eV for N1s and a weak peak at 531.4 eV for O1s the Oxygen group exist may be due to the water solvent (OH). Besides PIn/CDs showed two more crests at 398 eV and 532.2 eV corresponding to the N1s and O1s electronic configuration respectively. Fig. 5(b, c) showed an in-depth scan of the Cls electronic state, PIn showed two peaks at 283.5 eV and 284.7 eV corresponding to CC/CC and CN bonds, respectively [23]. In PIn/CDs, C1speak might be deconvoluted into four peaks at 282.3, 238, 284.1, and 286 eV accredited to the aromatic sp2 (CC/CH) type carbon, CN, CO/COOH, and the archetypal aromatic (π−π*) satellite peak [24,25]. Therefore, C1s core level XPS spectrum PIn/CDs film consists of the features of both pristine PIn and CDs. The deconvolution of N1s electronic state is exposed in Fig. 5(d, e) PIn illustrated the two N2 components such as NC and NH which is confirmed by the peaks at 397.4 eV and 400.2 eV. PIn/CDs films exhibit the pyrolytic ``N'' peaks like pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and graphitic N peaks confirmed by the peaks at 397.3, 397.8, and 398.5 eV [26]. The deconvoluted O1s electronic state is illustrated in Fig. 5(d). The O1s spectra showed three major peaks at 528.8, 529.9, and 530.1 eV corresponding to the CO, COH, and COC respectively, the above results confirmed the CDs are embedded into the polymer matrix [26]. The carbon and nitrogen-containing oxygen for PIn/CDs are slightly shifted from those in the spectrum of pristine PIn, due to the interaction between the PIn matrix and CDs. The change in the chemical environment pays to the detected shift in peak sites and intensities [27]. It confirmed under some conditions, these annotations were agreeable with FTIR analysis.

SEM images of the synthesized PIn, PCDs-1, PCDs-2, and PCDs-3 are shown in Fig. 6 . The SEM images of Pristine PIn showed a smooth surface; there is no aggregation and roughness taking place, which denotes that the PIn is evenly deposited on Ni-foam, as shown in Fig. 6(a,b). PCDs-1 is evenly deposited on the Ni foam skeletons and the fashioned PCDs-1 layer/film looks like wrinkled skin due to the addition of CDs it dramatically reformed the morphology shown in Fig. 6(c,d) [28]. The vertically packed nanoflakes on Ni foam formed a 3D porous morphology attained by the deposition of PCDs-2. The optimum addition of CDs into the polymer matrix augments the interconnection between the matrix surface and surface roughness, resulting in enhanced ionic interaction (Figs. 8 and 9). Further, the surface morphology of the film is deformed due to CDs, improving the flake-like structures as shown in Fig. 6(e,f). Fig. 6(g,h) displays an SEM image of PCDs-3 showing a rough surface due to the aggregation of polymer deposition [29,30].

Fig. 7 (a) shows the TEM images of PIn/CDs films, which show that the CDs are embedded into the polymer matrix (Fig. 7(b)) without any aggregation. Based on the TEM analysis, the morphology and average size of CDs are spherical in shape, and the average size is ∼ 1.6 nm. Furthermore, we also perceive low contrast floc around the CDs. These fuzzy areas are attributed to the PIn polymer matrix. The majority of CDs are embedded into the PIn matrix, which is revealed in Fig. 7(b). The enlarged view of spherical-shaped CDs embedded PIn matrix is illustrated in Fig. 7(c).

The electrochemical performance of PIn and PIn/CDs composites was analyzed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) with a potential window of 0–0.6 V. Fig. 8 (a), showed the CV curves of all electrodes at 80 mV/s, PCDs-2 showed the highest performance compare to all other electrodes. The redox shape of the CV curve denotes the faradic reactions between the electrode and electrolyte. The enclosed area of the CV curve is increased for PIn/CDs composites compared to the pristine PIn matrix, which denotes that the addition of CDs enhances the electrochemical performance of the composites due to the synergic effects between the PIn and CDs. The low electrical conductivity and instability of PIn limited the electrochemical performance of the pristine polymer matrix when the CDs were incorporated into the polymer matrix which electrostatically combined with the polymer surface. The Surface functionalities of CDs react with the polymer matrix and enhance the specific surface area (roughness and porosity) and shorten the ion/electron diffusion pathway of the PIn matrix, which offers more ion storage, resulting in a high capacitive performance of PCDs Films. The CDs surface passivated heteroatoms induce the electrochemical performance of the polymer matrix owing to the conjugation of Oxygen and Nitrogen loan pair electrons, and the π conjugation of the polymer matrix decreases the electrode resistance and improves the electrode/electrolyte interference.
This functionality enhances the reactive sites in the polymer composites which could enhance the specific capacity of the electrode material.

The possible redox mechanism of nitrogen (N2) containing compounds in an alkali medium is given in Eqs. (1) and (2) [31]. *C denotes the carbon network
(1) *C–NH2 + 2OH− ↔ *CNH + 2H2O + 2e−
(2) *C–NH2 + 2OH− ↔ *C–NHOH + H2O + 2e−

Galvanic charge-discharge (GCD) profiles of all electrodes at 10A/g have been shown in Fig. 8(b), CS can be calculated by using the following Eq. (3).
(3)
Q S
=
I ×
Δ
t
/
m
C / g
where I - current (mA), ∆t - discharge time (s), V -potential window (V), and m – active-material mass (mg).

PCDs-2 showed the highest QS of 397 C/g at 6 A/g in 3 M KOH, which is higher than others, PIn, PCDs-1, and PCDs-3 show QS of 46, 155.3, and 208.5 C/g respectively. The QS decreases with increasing current density due to the minimal chance of intercalation and de-intercalation of electrode materials and electrolyte ions.PCDs-3 shows lower QS than PCDs-2 due to the high quantity of CDs and increase dinternal resistance that reduces the electrochemical performance of PCDs-3. The CDs enhance the pores and specific surface area with the heteroatom functionality which contributes to enhancing the specific capacity of the electrode materials upto a certain concentration (PCDs-2) after a certain concentration of CDs addition attributed to the aggregation on the surface, as well as the higher concentration of CDs increase the internal resistance in the polymer matrix due to these aspects the PCDs shows lower electrochemical performance than PCDs-2.The QS comparison of all synthesized electrodes is shown in Fig. 8(c). The QS of a prepared electrode falls from high current density to low current density, and the rate capability for PIn, PCDs-1, PCDs-2, and PCDs-3 is attained to be 53%, 77.5%, 80.6%, and 77.9%at the highest current density of 20 A/g. So, which is shows the incorporation of CDs enhances the specific capacity of the electrode material. The specific capacitance of the synthesized samples was compared with previous literature (Table 2 ). The voltage is applied across the supercapacitor terminals during the charging process. The current flows into the SCs and is stored on the electrode materials (whether the faradic or non-faradic process takes place). Initially, the charging rate is high and decreases as it approaches the applied voltage. The current is decreased at a high potential difference, which decreases the electron flow rate. Under the discharging process, the stored energy is released from the SCs. Initially, the discharge current is high, but it is decreasing as the SCs stored energy is depleted.

Fig. 8(d) showed the coulombic-efficiency and cyclic-retention plots of PCDs-2, it showed retention of 75% after 3000 cycles. Nyquist plots of PIn and PIn/CDs composite electrodes are revealed in Fig. 8(e). The pristine PIn electrode showed an erect line in the low-frequency region screening an idyllic capacitive performance unified with low-charge transfer resistance. The semicircle diameter has been considered as a charge-transfer, electron transfer rate between electrolyte and electrode at their electrochemical boundary at the high frequencies [40]. It indicated the PCDs-2 has lower resistance compared to other electrode composites. Rs and Rct values of the prepared electrodes are listed in Table 3 , they showed the PCDs-2 offered low Rs and Rct values due to the high electrical conductivity, and the ratio of CDs increases the surface area of the electrode so the electrolyte ions easily access the electrode surface. The equivalent circuit diagram of the PCDS-2 electrode is revealed in Fig. 8(f). The Boat impedance Plot is illustrated in Fig. 7(g, h) it showed the resistance is least at a higher frequency region and shows an inclined line with slopes at a lower frequency, signifying the capacitive performance of as-prepared materials. The phase-angle is directly propositional to the concentration of CDs as illustrated in Fig. 8(h) which signified the synthesized materials store the electric energy based on both mechanism of EDLC and pseudocapacitive manner. The phase angle of as-prepared electrode materials is 55, 62, 62.8, and 63.5θ corresponding to PIn, PCDS-1, PCDS-2, and PCD-3 respectively. The CDs enhanced the ionic diffusion rate from the electrode-electrolyte interface which increase the specific capacity and life span of the electrode material by decreasing the ionic transference resistance. Hence, the peak detected in the high-frequency area is denoted ϕ. based on the CDs concentration from 0.1 to 0.2 w% the peak move towards the high-frequency region, which decreases the ion transference resistance of the electrode materials which offers more reactive sites for electrolyte ion interaction and enhances the specific capacity and its life-spam. At high CDs, concentration aggregates into the polymer matrix and increases the ionic diffusing resistance rate [41–43]. Therefore the ϕ peak of 0.3 W% of CDS (PCDS-3) is almost analogous to the pristine PIn polymer matrix which indicates the increasing resistance will decrease the specific capacity of the PCDS-3 composite. This can be confirmed with the support of the following analysis (CV and GCD).

The photography images of the synthesized electrodes' bending and non-bending images are revealed in Fig. 9 (a–c). The flexible property of the as-prepared electrode is analyzed by CV, GCD, and EIS spectra (Fig. 8(d-g)). The CV spectra are shown in Fig. 9(d) at various bending angles of 0°, 90°, and 180° under the constant scan rate of 50 mV/s, it showed the CV enclosed area is decreased during the twisting angles compared to 0° or plate state. This may be due to the increased internal resistance of the electrode material and the bending angles reduce the interfacial contact between the electrode and electrolyte solution. For the quantitative measurements, GCD analysis was performed which is illustrated in Fig. 9(e). The specific capacity was calculated for different bending angles, for 0° offers a high specific capacity compare to other bending angles, and 320, 275, and 271 C/g at 12 A/g corresponding to the bending angles of 0°, 90°, and 180° (Fig. 9(f)), respectively. The bending angles (0°, 90°, and 180°) could not highly affect the specific capacity of the electrode, which showed capacity retention of 85.9 and 84.6% for the bending angles of 90° and 180°. EIS spectra are also one of the supportive studies for the above discussion which is illustrated in Fig. 9(g). This manifests that the contact resistance and solution resistance of 0° is lesser than that of 90° and 180° bending angles, the magnified view of EIS spectra is inset in Fig. 9(g). The bode plots of various bending angles are illustrated in Fig. 9(h,i) a phase angle of 59, 52, and 43 corresponding to the bending angle of 0°, 90°, and 180°respectively. The ϕ peak at the high-frequency region is migrated into the low-frequency region which indicated the increased ion transference resistance of the electrode. Hence, the bending angles (0°, 90°, and 180°) moderately increase the diffusion resistance which slightly affects the specific capacity of the electrode material. The above results proposed the synthesized PCD electrodes are semi-flexible.

ASC device is fabricated by PCDs-2 as an anode(positive) and reduced graphene oxide(rGO) as a cathode(negative) electrode, filter paper is used as a separate with PVA/KOH polymer gel electrolyte. According to the mass balance theory, the mass ratio of the cathode and anode was examined using the following Eq. (4).
(4)
m +
m −
=
Q
g −
Q
g +

Where: m is the mass of active material in gram (g), Qg- and Qg+ are the specific capacity of negative and positive electrodes. The mass ratio (m +/m −) of active materials was examined. The overall mass of the anode and cathode is 1 mg and 2 mg, respectively. The schematic illustration of PCDs-2//rGO device fabrication is revealed in Fig. 10 (a),the potential window of ASC is calculated from PCDs-2 and rGO CV curve as displayed in Fig. 10(b), and the potential window for PCDs-2 is 0–0.6 V and rGO is −1.0–0 V, the operating potential of PCDs-2//rGO is extended up to 1.6 V.The CV curves of PCDs-2//rGO with various potential windowsat a scan rate of 10 mV s−1, the operating potential window of PCDs-2//rGOis 1.45 V. Fig. 10(c) showed the CV curves of PCDs-2//rGO device with a constant scan rate of 80 mV/s under different potential. The GCD profile of the fabricated device was performed under different potentials with a constant current density of 3 A/g which is illustrated in Fig. 10(d).

GCD profiles of PCDs//rGO at different current density from 1 to 10 A/g is revealed in Fig. 11 (a).The specific capacity of the fabricated device is examined using the following Eq. (5).
(5)
C =
2
× I ×
Δ
t
/
m
C / g

The fabricated PCDs-2//rGO device displays almost symmetric charge/discharge curves, signifying good storage ability. PCDs-2//rGO showed a specific capacity of 54.5 C/g at 1 A/g (Fig 11(b)). The energy and power density can be calculated using the following Eqs. (6) and (7) respectively.
(6)
E =
C
Δ
V
2
2
×
3.6
W h / k g
(7)
P =
3600
× E
Δ
t
W / k g
Where: E – energy density, P – power density, V – potential window, and t- discharge time.

PCDs//rGO showed a high energy density of 15.9 Wh/Kg and a high-power density of 955.6 W/Kg. Fig. 11(c), displays the energy and power density compared with previous reports (Rangone plot), of Graphene/MnO2 (6.8 Wh/kg @ 62 W/kg) [44], PANI/rGO (8.3 Wh/kg @ 60 W/kg) [45], MNR-rGO (15.4 Wh/kg @ 436.5 W/kg) [45], G-mPANI (11.3 Wh/kg@ 106.7 W/kg) [46], PVA–KOH–KI (7.8 Wh/kg @ 15.34 KW/kg) [47]. The PCDs-2//rGO device offers good cyclic reliability of 70.1% after 3000 continuous charging/discharging cycles at the current density of 20 A/g revealed in Fig. 11(d).The fitted equivalent circuit of the PCDs-2//rGO device is illustrated in Fig. 11(e). The Nyquist plot of the fabricated device is shown in Fig. 11(f) it denotes the internal resistance of the fabricated PCDS-2//rGO device. The fabricated device had shown the Rs and Rct value of 0.4746 Ω and 0.878 Ω and there is no semi-circle attained at the high-frequency area, signifying the in significant ionic transference resistance. The above results denoted that the PCDs-2 is a potential candidate for supercapacitor application.

A hybrid electrospray technique has been employed to make ultra-thin CDs interpenetrated PIn (PIn/CDs) film. The reinforced CDs not only increase the mass of the film but also enhance the electrochemical performance of the PIn matrix. The resulting QS of PCDs-2 is 394 C/g at 6 A/g in the three-electrode system and it shows good cyclic retention of 75% at 20 A/g. The fabricated PCDs-2//rGO device offers a specific capacity of 54.5 C/g at 1 A/g with cyclic retention of 70.1% at 20 A/g. PCDs//rGO shows an energy density of 15.9 Wh/kg at a power density of 955.6 W/kg. These fascinating results demonstrate great potential in emerging high energy/power density PCDs-2//rGO ASC for real-world applications.

Elumalai Dhandapani: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Kavitha kandiah: Writing – review & editing. R Vasudevan: Writing – review & editing. R Ramesh: Writing – review & editing. Navaneethan Duraisamy: Investigation, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Navaneethan Duraisamy acknowledges the DST, New Delhi, India, for the DST Inspire Faculty award (DST/INSPIRE/04/ 2018/001444).
